conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have three children actually. There's Alex who's 7, Charlie who's also 7, and Blake who's 20. I'm hoping to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I assess your eligibility correctly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information about Alex (7), Charlie (7), and Blake (20). Now I need to ask you a few questions to properly assess eligibility for each child.

Let me start with Blake, who is 20. Is Blake currently in any approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't studying at the moment, no. He's actually working full-time at a warehouse. Been there for about a year now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask about Alex and Charlie, who are both 7 years old.

Do Alex and Charlie live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just to confirm a few more details:

Are you living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I live in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Just one more question to complete the assessment:

Are either Alex or Charlie currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or living with someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, neither of them are in hospital or anything like that. They're both at home with me. 

Oh, actually I should mention - I am fostering Alex. But I don't receive any money from the council for looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. That's very helpful information.

Just to make sure I have all the details correct - you mentioned you don't receive any money from the council for fostering Alex. Does the council pay anything at all towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nothing at all. I cover all of Alex's costs myself - the council doesn't contribute anything towards his accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children.

Let me evaluate each child's situation:

**Alex (7 years old):** You are fostering Alex, he lives with you, you're in the UK, and the local council is not paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance. According to the rules, you can get Child Benefit for a foster child as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.

**Charlie (7 years old):** Charlie is 7, lives with you in the UK, and you're responsible for him. Children under 16 qualify for Child Benefit when you're responsible for them and they live with you.

**Blake (20 years old):** Blake is 20 years old and working full-time. Child Benefit normally stops when a child turns 16, unless they stay in approved education or training (which can extend it until they're under 20). Since Blake is not in approved education or training and is working full-time, he does not qualify.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Charlie**.

Here's the breakdown for each child:

✅ **Alex (age 7)** - **Eligible**
Even though you're fostering Alex, you can claim Child Benefit for him because the local council isn't paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance. Since he lives with you and you cover all his costs yourself, you qualify.

✅ **Charlie (age 7)** - **Eligible**
Charlie is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you're responsible for him, so you meet all the requirements for Child Benefit.

❌ **Blake (age 20)** - **Not Eligible**
Unfortunately, Blake doesn't qualify because he's 20 years old, working full-time, and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit can continue past age 16 only if the young person is under 20 and in approved education or training.

You can apply for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie. Please note that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit eligibility?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
